title: PCBWay order: thickness is 1.6 mm, should be 1.0 mm
description: The PCBWay configurator screenshot (pcbway-selection.png) has 1.6 mm highlighted. The README specifies 1.0 mm, and the 3D-printed enclosure's PCB cutout expects a 1 mm board. Boards ordered at 1.6 mm will not fit the case.
resolution: In the PCBWay order form, change the thickness to 1.0 mm before checkout.

title: PCBWay order: HASL (Hot Air Solder Leveling) with lead, should be lead-free HASL or ENIG (gold-plated finish)
description: The PCBWay configurator screenshot shows 'HASL with lead' selected. The README specifies 'lead-free HASL finish' (or ENIG). Leaded HASL is RoHS-noncompliant (contains restricted hazardous substances under EU regulations) — matters for EU shipping, some resellers, and kit distribution.
resolution: In the PCBWay order form, switch surface finish to 'Lead-Free HASL' (or upgrade to ENIG).

title: C2 e-paper driver assembly (PCBA) cost is missing from every BOM
description: None of the three BOMs (COGS-LIST, OpenBook_BOM_Clean PDF, April 2025 image) include the fabrication/assembly cost of the Castellated E-Paper Driver (OSO-BOOK-C2). This is a separate pre-assembled board (PCBA) job from either JLCPCB or PCBWay. Real per-unit cost is likely $50-60, not the $43-48 printed on the BOMs.
resolution: Get an assembly (PCBA) quote from JLCPCB (using OSO-BOOK-C2-03 files) or PCBWay (using OSO-BOOK-C2-02 files). Typical 10-unit small-board assembly runs $30-80 depending on part sourcing.

title: why-the-open-book describes the ESP32-S3 design, not the Pi Pico build
description: The vision doc 'why-the-open-book' opens with 'designed around the ESP32-S3' and describes a slim LiPo battery. The current DIY build uses the Raspberry Pi Pico (RP2040) with dual AAA. The ESP32-S3 design has since become the Open Book Touch — a manufactured product heading to Crowd Supply with a 4.26-inch touch display, front light, and USB-C charging. The vision doc describes neither the Pico build nor the Touch; it's the project's origin story.
resolution: Treat the vision doc as historical context, not a spec. The Pico-based Abridged Edition (what this app covers) and the ESP32-S3-based Open Book Touch are now two separate product lines.

title: COGS-LIST references an SRAM chip that doesn't exist on C1
description: COGS-LIST line 120 mentions 'r12 is our pull-up for the sram chip'. There is no SRAM chip on the Pi Pico main board — the Pico has 264 KB of SRAM internal to the RP2040. This note was transcribed from the older ESP32-S3 (B1) design.
resolution: Ignore the SRAM annotation for C1. R12 on C1, if present in the schematic, is not an SRAM pull-up.

title: COGS-LIST references audio hardware that doesn't exist on C1
description: COGS-LIST mentions a headphone jack, 100 µF audio cap, audio ferrite beads, and R6/R7/R8/R9 biasing resistors for headphone output. None of these components are on the Pi Pico main board — they're from the older ESP32-S3 (B1) design which added audio.
resolution: Ignore all audio-related annotations for the C1 build. If you want audio, that's a custom expansion via the JST ports.

title: COGS-LIST MOSFET line: $0.32 shown where $6.40 belongs (20 × $0.32)
description: COGS-LIST lists P-MOSFETs at '$0.32' with quantity 20, but the line-total shown is $0.32 rather than the correct $6.40 (20 × $0.32). The total 'Estimated Total for 10 Units: $432.89' is therefore ~$6 lower than it should be.
resolution: Use the OpenBook_BOM_Clean PDF totals ($460.69 / 10 units) which have this arithmetic correct. Or mentally add $6 to the COGS-LIST total.

title: April 2025 BOM image omits the Keystone 1022C battery retainer
description: The April 2025 BOM screenshot shows 12 line items with 'Estimated Cost per Unit: $43.26'. It omits the Keystone 1022C retainer clip that appears in both the OpenBook_BOM_Clean PDF and COGS-LIST. Without the clip, AAA cells are not held in place reliably.
resolution: Add Keystone 1022C (Digi-Key 36-1022C-ND) to any order based on this BOM. Adds $0.86/unit.

title: COGS-LIST labels Q1 as 'M channel MOSFET' — should be 'P channel'
description: COGS-LIST line 150 says 'Q1 - M channel Mosfet'. The DMG3415U-7 listed in the BOM is a P-channel MOSFET. 'M' is almost certainly a typo for 'P'. (The C2 driver module does have an N-channel IRLML0100 for its boost converter, but that's a different board.)
resolution: Read 'M channel' as 'P channel' wherever it appears in COGS-LIST for the C1 board.

title: GDEW042T2 (EOL) vs GDEY042T81 — different controller ICs, not a simple relabel
description: The README and 1-click-bom.csv list the display as GDEW042T2, which uses the UC8176 (IL0398) controller IC. COGS-LIST and the April 2025 BOM say GDEY042T81, which uses the SSD1683 controller. These are physically pin-compatible (same FPC connector and form factor) but require different firmware drivers. GDEW042T2 is now EOL at GoodDisplay — new builders will almost certainly receive GDEY042T81 panels.
resolution: The libros firmware has drivers for both controllers (OpenBook_IL0398 for GDEW042T2, OpenBook_SSD1683 for GDEY042T81), but the pre-built book.uf2 on the project site targets IL0398 only. If you receive a GDEY042T81, you will need to build libros from source or use the branch from PR #11 (joeycastillo/libros#11) which adds auto-detection. Check your display's controller IC before flashing.

title: Pico SKU: SC0915 vs SC0918 — both are the same Pico
description: COGS-LIST and the April 2025 BOM use SC0915; 1-click-bom.csv uses SC0918. These are two Raspberry Pi distributor SKUs for the same Pico board. Either works.
resolution: No action needed — whichever Pico Digi-Key has in stock is fine.

title: COGS-LIST says 12 caps for the boost circuit; real count is 10
description: COGS-LIST line 139 refers to 'BIG LINE 12 ONE MICROFARAD CAPACITORS' for the boost circuit. The actual C2-02 BOM has 9× 1 µF + 1× 4.7 µF = 10 caps total on the e-paper driver module. The count was approximate or describes an earlier C2 revision.
resolution: Trust the C2-02 BOM over the COGS-LIST annotation for cap counts on the driver module.

title: JLCPCB driver module is now C2-03, not C2-01 — the README is outdated
description: The upstream repo's Fabrication Files directory contains 'OSO-BOOK-C2-03 (JLCPCB)' with gerbers, BOM, and PNP files ready for JLCPCB's PCBA service. The older C2-01 fabrication folder no longer exists in the repo. However, the README still tells builders to use 'OSO-BOOK-C2-01' for JLCPCB orders, and the 1-click-bom.csv lists U2 as 'OSO-BOOK-C2-01'. C2-03 is the same circuit as C2-01 (identical schematic) but uses the smaller board outline from C2-02, with R1 back to 0402 and JLCPCB-compatible design rules.
resolution: If ordering the C2 driver from JLCPCB, use the OSO-BOOK-C2-03 fabrication files (gerbers, BOM, PNP) from the upstream repo's Fabrication Files directory. Ignore the README's reference to C2-01.

title: C1 gerber: the repo has both C1-04 and C1-05, but only C1-04 is documented
description: The README instructs builders to upload 'OSO-BOOK-C1-04-rounded.zip' to their PCB fab. However, the repo also contains 'OSO-BOOK-C1-05-rounded.zip' in the same Fabrication Files directory, and the machine-edited kitspace.yaml points to the C1-05 file as the canonical gerber. The schematic header says 'Revision: 05'. C1-05 is likely the current revision, but the README was never updated.
resolution: Use OSO-BOOK-C1-05-rounded.zip for new orders — the kitspace.yaml and schematic revision header both point to C1-05 as current. The C1-04 zip is still present as a fallback.

title: BOM specifies TL1107AF180WQ (1.8 N) but the README links to 130WQ (1.3 N)
description: The 1-click-bom.csv and the Digi-Key part number (EG2511-ND) both specify TL1107AF180WQ — the 180 g (1.8 N) actuation force variant. The README's Digi-Key link, however, goes to TL1107AF130WQ (1.3 N, lighter press). This is an inconsistency within the upstream project itself. Either variant fits the same footprint.
resolution: Either variant works. The BOM's 180WQ (1.8 N) gives a firmer click; the README's 130WQ (1.3 N) gives a lighter touch. Order whichever you prefer — the 180WQ is what the BOM auto-orders via Digi-Key.
